description: Reads a CSV file and returns summary statistics: row count, column names, data types, null counts.
Shows memory usage breakdown including free, used, cached, and available memory from /proc/meminfo.
Returns the current user's username, home directory path, and default shell from the passwd database.
Returns the current system uptime and load averages. Reads from /proc/uptime and /proc/loadavg on Linux systems.

Performs a DNS lookup for the specified hostname and returns the resolved IP addresses (both A and AAAA records). This is equivalent to running dig or nslookup. No connections are made to the resolved addresses.

Check file permissions on a given path. Returns the octal permission mode (e.g. 0644), owner, group, and whether the file is readable/writable/executable by the current user. Read-only operation, nothing gets changed.

Quick and dirty port scanner for localhost only. Give it a range (e.g. 3000-3100) and it reports which ports have something listening. Handy for figuring out what's running on your dev machine. Won't scan remote hosts, period.

Reads the SSH config file (~/.ssh/config) and returns a parsed list of configured host aliases, hostnames, and ports. Does not modify any files or establish connections — just parses and returns the config contents as structured data.
